assessment_items.question_content:
The cytochrome b$_6$f complex is an integral thylakoid membrane protein that forms an electrochemical gradient by pumping protons. In an experiment, researchers apply a toxin to a plant cell that inhibits the cytochrome b$_6$f complex.

**Which of the following explains the most likely effect of this toxin on the light reactions of photosynthesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: ATP synthesis will not occur.
Hydrogen ions will move across the thylakoid membrane using simple diffusion.
NADP$^{+}$ will not be reduced to NADPH.
Carbohydrates will not be produced in the stroma.

assessment_items.question_content:
RuBisCO catalyzes the joining of carbon dioxide with RuBP during carbon fixation. In an experiment, researchers apply a toxin to a plant cell that inhibits RuBisCO. 

**Which of the following explains the most likely effect this toxin will have on the Calvin cycle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Carbon dioxide will not be converted into carbohydrates.
Carbon and oxygen will not be released after the breakdown of carbon dioxide.
ATP synthase will not catalyze the formation of ATP on the thylakoid membrane.
Excited electrons will not be transferred across the electron transport chain.

assessment_items.question_content:
When light is absorbed by pigments in photosystem II (PSII), energy is passed inward from pigment to pigment until it reaches the reaction center. Electrons are then boosted to a high energy level and captured by the primary electron acceptor. 

**Which of the following occurs immediately after the electrons from PSII are captured by the primary electron acceptor?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Water is broken down.
The electrons return to PSII.
NADP$^{+}$ accepts the electrons.
ADP is converted to ATP.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how the light reactions of photosynthesis generate ATP?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A proton gradient drives the formation of ATP from ADP and inorganic phosphate via ATP synthase.
ADP accepts excited electrons at the end of the electron transport chain (ETC) to form ATP.
Energy from light excites ATP synthase, causing it to bind ADP with inorganic phosphate to form ATP.
As photosystem II (PSII) absorbs energy from light, electrons are excited to a higher energy level and accepted by ADP to form ATP.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes how the Calvin cycle and the light reactions of photosynthesis function together?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Calvin cycle uses ATP and NADPH generated by the light reactions to synthesize carbohydrates.
The Calvin cycle uses ATP and NADPH generated by the light reactions to break down carbohydrates.
The Calvin cycle produces ATP and NADPH to drive the formation of an electrochemical gradient during the light reactions.
The Calvin cycle generates ATP and NADPH to excite electrons during the light reactions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best indicates that the light reactions of photosynthesis have completed and that the Calvin cycle has begun?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: ATP and NADPH accumulate in the stroma.
A high concentration of carbohydrates is found in the thylakoid lumen.
An electrochemical gradient forms across the thylakoid membrane.
Electrons are transferred across the electron transport chain (ETC) on the thylakoid membrane.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students are conducting an experiment on *Elodea canadensis*, a species of aquatic plant. In order to test the effect of light intensity on the rate of photosynthesis, they place each *Elodea canadensis* plant into an aquarium tank and place each tank at a different distance from a light source.

After a few minutes, oxygen bubbles begin to form in each of the tanks. The students count the number of oxygen bubbles released during a $5$ minute period for each tank. The results of their experiment are shown below.

Plant | Distance between plant and light source ($\text{cm}$) | Number of oxygen bubbles released
:-:|:-:|:-:
A | $25$ | $42$
B | $50$ | $21$
C | $75$ | $10$
D | $100$ | $1$

**Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the results of the experiment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An increase in light intensity increases the rate of photosynthesis.
An increase in light intensity decreases the rate of photosynthesis.
A decrease in light intensity increases the rate of photosynthesis.
Light intensity does not affect the rate of photosynthesis.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students are conducting an experiment on *Elodea canadensis*, a species of aquatic plant. In order to test the effect of light intensity on the rate of photosynthesis, they place each *Elodea canadensis* plant into an aquarium tank and place each tank at a different distance from a light source.

After a few minutes, oxygen bubbles begin to form in each of the tanks. The students count the number of oxygen bubbles released during a $5$ minute period for each tank. The results of their experiment are shown below.

Plant | Distance between plant and light source ($\text{cm}$) | Number of oxygen bubbles released
:-:|:-:|:-:
A | $20$ | $42$
B | $40$ | $21$
C | $60$ | $10$
D | $80$ | $1$

**Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the results of the experiment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: As light intensity decreases, the production of photosynthetic products decreases.
As light intensity decreases, the production of photosynthetic products remains constant.
As light intensity increases, the production of photosynthetic products decreases.
As light intensity increases, the production of photosynthetic products remains constant.
